coredata.dc:title: Developed a ratiometric fluorescence pH nanosensor based on label-free carbon dots for intracellular lysosome imaging and water pH monitoring with a smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Songrui
Song, Xuerong
Wang, Yuhan
Hu, Zhiru
Yan, Fei
Feng, Guodong
coredata.dc:description: It is necessary to measure lysosomal pH in living cells in order to maintain the normal performance of cell functions. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor based on carbon dots for lysosomal pH sensing was successfully developed via one-pot solvothermal means. The CDs have two pH-dependent emission wavelengths locating at 415 and 555 nm with distinct fluorescence color change switching between blue and yellow from pH 3.0 to 8.0. Due to its plentiful amino groups on the surface, the probe is lysosome-targeted. It is demonstrated that intensity ratios (F555/F415) against pH = 3.0–8.0 are fitted with the Boltzmann equation with a pKa of 5.47 matched well with lysosomal pH. Because of the merits of great pH sensitivity, selectivity, biocompatibility and photostability, the probe was successfully applied for lysosomal pH detection during cells imaging. Besides, CDs were further used for acidic and neutral water pH sensing by a smartphone in a fast and expedient way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lysosome targeting
Ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor
Smartphone
pH monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing procedure of water pH by a smartphone
Selectivity, stability and reversibility of the nanosensor to pH
Detection of water pH by a smartphone

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to acknowledge Professor Zhang Kai and Associate Professor Hou Changmin, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, China, for their assistance with the TEM, DLS and XPS in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and structure of nanosensor. (A) TEM image. Inset: the diameter distribution. (B) HRTEM image.
(A) XPS full-scan spectra of CDs; and the XPS high resolution spectrum of (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, and (D) O 1s.

(A) Photoluminescence of the nanosensor in BR buffer of pH = 3–8. Inset: an illustration of color variation exposed to 365 nm UV light. (B) Relationship between the ratios (F555/F415) and the pH values. λex = 365 nm.
(A) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F555/F415) of CDs at pH = 5.0 and 7.0 containing 0.1 mM interferences. (B) Reversibility against pH between 3.0 and 8.0.
Colocalization images of cells treated with 200 μg mL−1 carbon dots and Lysotracker Red.
Confocal fluorescence images of cells treated with 400 μg mL−1 CDs in buffer solution of different pH.
The relationship between the value of G/B and pH of water.
Synthesis of CDs and the application for lysosomal pH detecting.
Real samples detected by smartphone and ratiometric fluorescence detection compared with pH meter.

head.ce:title: Developed a ratiometric fluorescence pH nanosensor based on label-free carbon dots for intracellular lysosome imaging and water pH monitoring with a smartphone

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, International Joint Research Laboratory of Nano-Micro Architecture Chemistry (NMAC), International Research Center for Chemistry-Medicine Joint Innovation, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry
International Joint Research Laboratory of Nano-Micro Architecture Chemistry (NMAC)
International Research Center for Chemistry-Medicine Joint Innovation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, International Joint Research Laboratory of Nano-Micro Architecture Chemistry (NMAC), International Research Center for Chemistry-Medicine Joint Innovation, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is necessary to measure lysosomal pH in living cells in order to maintain the normal performance of cell functions. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor based on carbon dots for lysosomal pH sensing was successfully developed via one-pot solvothermal means. The CDs have two pH-dependent emission wavelengths locating at 415 and 555 nm with distinct fluorescence color change switching between blue and yellow from pH 3.0 to 8.0. Due to its plentiful amino groups on the surface, the probe is lysosome-targeted. It is demonstrated that intensity ratios (F555/F415) against pH = 3.0–8.0 are fitted with the Boltzmann equation with a pKa of 5.47 matched well with lysosomal pH. Because of the merits of great pH sensitivity, selectivity, biocompatibility and photostability, the probe was successfully applied for lysosomal pH detection during cells imaging. Besides, CDs were further used for acidic and neutral water pH sensing by a smartphone in a fast and expedient way.

ce:section.ce:para: pH in living cells is a momentous parameter that is associated with numerous biological processes such as cell proliferation and apoptosis [1], ion transport [2], endocytosis [3] and so on. Change of cellular pH can cause many diseases including rheumatoid arthritis, Alzheimer's disease and even cancer [4–6]. As is known to all, the internal pH value is diverse obviously in eukaryotic cells. For example, the pH range of the nucleus is 7.2–7.4, the Golgi is 6.4–6.8, the lysosome is 4.4–5.5 and the mitochondrial pH is about 8.0 [7–9]. Each sub-organelle has specific function and the abnormality of pH in organelles will lead to the disorder of the function [10]. As the most acidic organelle, lysosome is associated with the decomposition of biological macromolecules including protein, nucleic acid and polysaccharide, removal of some pathogens and other intracellular biological processes [11]. Abnormality of lysosomal pH can induce autophagy blockage and storage disorders [8]. Therefore, it's of great importance to design probes competent for monitoring lysosomal pH variation, not only to better understand organelle functions but also help with early diagnosis of disease [12].

Fluorescent probes based on organic dyes and fluorescent proteins have been extensively exploited for detecting intracellular pH on account of its high spatial and temporal resolution, noninvasiveness and real-time signals [13–15]. Regrettably, some of them present photobleaching, poor biocompatibility and demanding a tedious synthetic and purification process [16,17]. To meet these demands, many fluorescent nanoparticles based on silica [18] or gold [19], semiconductor quantum dots [20] and lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles [21] have been developed for intracellular pH sensing. Carbon dots (CDs), possessing numerous advantages of great biocompatibility, photostability, reversibility as well as ease of synthesis [22], have been also developed by more and more researchers. They can be easily synthesized from various precursors through bottom-up or top-down means and further applied in chemical quantification [23,24], bioimaging [25,26] and nanomedicine [27,28]. Attempts have been made to design ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors based on CDs for pH sensing in living cells which can well answer the drawbacks induced by many interferences leading to the lower reliability, such as the drift of excitation light source, biological autofluorescence and so on [12,17,29] compared to those with single emission [30–32]. To our knowledge, only one ratiometric fluorescence probe based on one-pot-synthesized CDs is lysosome-targeted [12].

It is well known that lysosome has the lowest pH in cells and CDs coated by sufficient amino groups can better target such acidic organelle [33]. Herein, o-phenylenediamine was employed as the precursor due to its abundant amino groups and anthranilic acid was used to form ratiometric sensor. Fluorescence probe based on label-free carbon dots for ratiometricly lysosomal pH sensing was successfully developed via one-pot solvothermal means (Scheme 1 ). The CDs show blue and yellow fluorescence emissions and both are pH-sensitive. Besides, emission intensity ratios (F555/F415) against pH from 3.0 to 8.0 are fitted with the Boltzmann equation resulted a pKa of 5.47 which is well suitable for lysosomal pH sensing. According to the results we measured, CDs are qualified for ratiometricly sensing the fluctuation of lysosomal pH with the merits of satisfactory pH selectivity, reversibility, photostability and low toxicity. What is more, the nanosensor can be further applied for the detection of acidic and neutral water pH by a smartphone in a fast and portable way.

Briefly, 0.035 g anthranilic acid and 0.690 g o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 30 mL ethanol and the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated up to 180 °C for 6 h. The solution was cooled naturally before the product was preliminarily filtered with a 0.45 μm microporous membrane to discard the nonfluorescent impurities and further purified with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 2 days. Finally, the brown solid was obtained by freeze-drying under vacuum. And the stock solution of probe (10 mg mL−1) was prepared by adding the product into distilled water.

MCF-7 cells were first cultivated in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) including 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in humidified condition for one day in a 96-well plate. Then, cells were treated with different concentrations of carbon dots for another 24 h. 10 μL Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was put into each sample and cultivated for additional 2 h. Finally, the absorption intensity at 450 nm was read by an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay plate.

MCF-7 cells were first incubated in a 6-well plate with sterile coverslips and allowed to adhere for 24 h in advance. Thereafter, the cells were cultured with 400 μg mL−1 CDs for additional 4 h and sequentially washed with PBS for thrice before the medium was substituted by high-K+ HEPES-buffered solution containing 5 μg mL−1 nigericin at various pH (4.0, 5.0, 6.0 and 7.0) which can facilitate interchange of H+/K+ and rapidly balance pH across cell membranes [17] for 30 min, respectively. At last, the intracellular ratiometric fluorescence imaging was conducted on an inverted microscopy. The emission signals were recorded from 400 to 440 nm of channel 1 excited at 405 nm. As for channel 2, the images were collected at 530–570 nm when excited at 405 nm.

As for the drug stimulation, cells were first treated with 200 μM of chloroquine (CQ) for 40 min which can increase lysosomal pH [34] and 1 μM dexamethasone (DEX) for 6 h to induce cellular apoptosis accompany with a decrease of pH [35], respectively. The cells subsequently were treated with 400 μg mL−1 nanosensor for 4 h at 37 °C after the removal of the medium. Finally, images were gained using the experimentation described above.

For lysosome colocalization test, MCF-7 cells were firstly cultivated with nanosensor (400 μg mL−1) for 4 h. After the removal of excess CDs, Lyso- Tracker Red (50 nM) were coexistent with cells for 60 min. Images were collected on an inverted microscope.

QY of CDs was calculated as the following formula:
∅ x
=
∅
s t
(
D x
/
D
s t
)
(
A
s t
/
A x
)
(
n x
/
n
s t
)
2
where, x: the CDs dissolved in water; st: quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4; n: the refractive index of the solvent; A: absorption intensity; D: area of fluorescence emission spectra; Quinine sulfate was applied as reference. [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing procedure of water pH by a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: 1800 μl Britton-Robinson (BR) solution with diverse pH values (pH 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0) were mixed with 200 μl stock solution of CDs. They were irradiated by 365 nm UV lamp and the color signals of samples were collected by the smartphone in the dark. The B and G values of images were read out by the application called Color Analyzer as it is shown in Figure. S1.

The morphology and structure of carbon dots were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As Fig. 1 A of TEM image revealed, nanosensor is spherical in shape and disperses well in aqueous solution with an average diameter of 6.16 nm measured by nano measurer as the inset of Fig. 1A. HRTEM image in Fig. 1B displays CDs have an obvious lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is agree with the basal spacing of (1120) of graphene [37].

Furthermore, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out to confirm the surface functional groups and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was to determine the elemental composition of nanosensor. It can be seen from Fig. 2 , there is a distinct absorption band at 3359 cm−1, which is belong to N–H stretching vibrations, and the band at 1383 cm−1 and 1617 cm−1 can be ascribed to C–N and CN, respectively, confirming there are sufficient groups containing N atom on the surface of CDs. The bands at 3436 and 1460 cm−1 corresponding to –OH and –CO groups prove the existence of carboxyl groups. Besides, CDs also possess C–C, CC and C–H groups, locating at 1276, 1504 and 748 cm−1, severally. For XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 A), there are three typical peaks at 295.8, 409.7 and 541.8 eV, suggesting the existence of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3B), four different peaks can be found at 284.7, 286.5, 288.5 and 289.0 eV, which are ascribed to CC/C–C, C–O, CO and O–CO. The deconvolution of the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) displays four peaks at 398.8, 399.6, 400.7 and 402.1 eV, responding to pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. Fig. 3D shows that the high-resolution O 1s spectra can be resolved into two distinct peaks at 532.1 and 533.6 eV, representing CO and O–CO bonds. According to the above results, the elemental compositions of CDs measured by XPS are consistent with FT-IR analysis, indicating that CDs are coated by abundant amino and carboxyl groups. Element analysis was also performed to show that nanosensor is consist of C (65.92%), N (25.15%), O (1.632%) and H (7.298%).

The optical characteristics of nanosensor were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence excitation spectrum. In Figure. S2A, the UV–vis spectroscopy of the probe manifests two different absorption peaks at around 278 and 450 nm severally. The band at 278 nm corresponds to π→ π* transitions of aromatic CC bond. And the peak located at 450 nm is the characteristic absorption of CO or CN in connection with the graphitic framework of CDs [38]. As shown in Figure. S2B, it emerged that there are two different emission peaks at 415 and 555 nm respectively and the position of these peaks does not shift upon different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 380 nm displaying the excitation-independent property of CDs. In order to guarantee that both emission bands can be excited preferably and simultaneously, 365 nm is chosen as the excitation wavelength. And the QY of carbon dots in aqueous solution is optimally measured to reach 9.32% in water (Fig. S3).

Fluorescence spectrum of CDs with dual emissions in BR buffer solutions of different pH levels was measured. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity at 415 nm exhibited a dramatic decline while that at 555 nm increases slightly from pH = 3.0 to pH = 8.0. Especially, an obvious fluorescence color alteration from blue to yellow can be easily recognized under 365 nm UV light from acid to neutral conditions (Fig. 4A, insert). Fig. 4B shows a Boltzmann equation: F555/F415 = 0.52897–0.49422/(1 + exp ((pH − 5.47)/0.43)) between emission intensity ratios of F555/F415 and pH figured out a pKa value of 5.47, well matched with pH in lysosome. The response to pH of CDs is predicted to be resulted from the protonation–deprotonation of the amino and carboxyl groups [33,36]. As shown in Figure. S4, the Zeta potential of CDs becomes more negative as pH increases which is in accordance with our assumption. Besides, the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs at various pH is also measured by dynamic light scattering. It can be seen in Figure. S5, the diameter of the CDs increases first and then decreases as the pH increases from 3.00 to 8.00. It reaches the largest diameter of 41.10 nm at pH = 6.0 which is likely due to the slight aggregation. And it can be proved from the zeta potentials of CDs at different pH values in Fig. S4 which show that the zeta potential of CDs is relative weak when pH is about 6.00 and the relative positively charged surface in acidic solution and negatively in alkaline condition can oppose aggregation through electrostatic repulsion [32]. All above consequences confirmed the promising applications of the nanosensor for lysosomal pH sensing in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reversibility of the nanosensor to pH
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the complex biological environments in cells, the effect of relevant interferences was determined at the condition of pH 5.0 and 7.0 such as some anions and cations, amino acids, cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy) and other substances. As it shown in Fig. 5 A, a negligible variation can be observed in the fluorescence intensity ratios of different conditions, demonstrating the satisfactory selectivity of CDs for intracellular pH monitoring. As for the stability of the probe, Figure. S6 depicted that emission intensity ratios of F555/F415 fluctuate in a negligible range in BR buffer solutions of various pH (5.0 and 7.0) when the fluorescence of CDs was measured every 4 min in about an hour, showing the eligible stability. Besides, the ionic-strength stability of CDs is also achieved. According to Figure. S7, fluorescence intensity ratio is hardly affected by NaCl aqueous, even with the concentration of 1.0 mol L−1. Meanwhile, the pH reversibility of CDs was estimated by adjusting the pH level using HCl and NaOH as modulators. The result shows (Fig. 5B) CDs are equipped with great reproducibility. Taken together, CDs are be competent for intracellular pH tracking in terms of satisfactory selectivity, reversibility and stability.

Prior of cells imaging, cytotoxicity test was carried out to analyze the biocompatibility of CDs. As described in Figure. S8, the cell viability shows an ignorable decline even though the nanosensor is up to 500 μg mL−1, confirming the low toxicity of this probe. In order to ensure whether this probe can principally aggregate in lysosome as expected, the colocalization assay was explored using Lysotracker Red, a commercially lysosome targeted probe. It is worth noting in Fig. 6 that CDs display an intermediate fluorescence color after merging blue and yellow channels on account of the lower pH in lysosome and greatly overlayed with Lysotracker (red), with a Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.88 (Figure. S9) showing the nanosensor can selectively target lysosome. Afterwards, we determined the ability of CDs to track pH in lysosome. MCF-7 cells were treated with HEPES buffer solution including 5 μg mL−1 nigericin at various pH (4.0, 5.0, 6.0 and 7.0) for 30 min. As it displayed in Fig. 7 , the blue fluorescence intensity of channel 1 continuously fades out while the yellow of channel 2 gradually enhances as pH increases from 4.0 to 7.0 and the trend between their relative average fluorescence intensity ratio (Fyellow/Fblue) and pH (Figure. S10) is consistent with the fluorescence spectra above. At the same time, the merged images of two channels exhibit an apparent fluorescence color alteration from blue to yellow.

We further explored the function of CDs to detect lysosomal pH during drug stimulation. MCF-7 cells were cultured with two kinds of stimuli, CQ and DEX. Compared with the control, it can be easily seen from Figure. S11 that when cells were treated with DEX, the merged image is bluish and the ratio between yellow and blue relative average fluorescence intensity also decreases (Figure. S12) as a result of the acidification. However, when cells were dealt with CQ which can increase lysosomal pH, the conclusion is just the opposite and the merged image is yellowish. The above results of cell imaging proved that the CDs can not only monitor intracellular pH values but also can recognize the lysosomal pH fluctuation when cells exposed to various drug stimuli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water pH by a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the two emission peaks located at 415 and 555 nm of CDs, which are well overlayed with the typical response of the Smartphone G and B channels (green and blue) [39], the CDs were further applied for acidic and neutral water pH sensing by smartphone in a convenient and fast way. As it can be seen in Fig. 8 , the ratios of G/B versus pH 3.0–7.0 follow the Boltzmann equation:G/B = 1.21981–0.70465/(1 + exp ((pH − 5.07)/0.39)). Besides, using two signal values gained by professional image processing software to detect pH can avoid many interference factors, such as the ambient environment or the type of smartphone when taken photos [40]. It can be proved in Table. S1, the values of G/B read from images gained by iPhone XR and HUAWEI P40 Pro respectively are almost similar.

To further determine the feasibility of the proposed way, mineral water and snow water were selected as real samples. As it revealed in Table 1 , the data gained by smartphone were comparable to those from commercial electrochemical pH meter and ratiometric fluorescence detection, revealing the practicality of this smartphone-based method.

When compared with some well-known pH sensing dyes including fluorescein, rhodamine, fluorescein isothiocyanate and others, CDs obtained here have better water solubility, biocompatibility and photostability in spite of lower fluorescence quantum yield [41]. Besides, some reported probes for intracellular pH detection based on carbon dots have been compared. As it is shown in Table. S2, the carbon dots-based nanosensor developed here can not only ratiometricly monitor intracellular pH but also can aggregate in lysosome without further decoration. What is more, it can be further applied for fast and portable detection of acidic and natural water pH by a smartphone.

In conclusion, a new ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor based on carbon dots for lysosomal pH monitoring was developed through one-pot solvothermal means. Employing anthranilic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, carbon dots display two pH-sensitive emissions at 415 and 555 nm and their ratios (F555/F415) versus pH 3.0–8.0 are fitted with the Boltzmann equation yielded a pKa of 5.47 which is suitable for lysosomal pH sensing. The sensor is determined to be qualified for lysosomal pH detection during drug stimulation and colocalization test. CDs were further used in acidic and natural water pH detecting by smartphone in a fast and convenient way. Hence, we believe that this carbon dots-based nanosensor may have promising applications in bioimaging and real sample test fields.

Songrui Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Xuerong Song: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yuhan Wang: Data curation, Visualization. Zhiru Hu: Visualization, Investigation. Fei Yan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Guodong Feng: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge Professor Zhang Kai and Associate Professor Hou Changmin, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, China, for their assistance with the TEM, DLS and XPS in this work.
